perturbation.original_text: 2.5 The Receiving Party agrees to use such reasonable measures necessary and standard of care to protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information from any misuse or misappropriation by any party as the Receiving Party would apply to its own confidential information and agrees to notify the Disclosing Party, to the extent permitted under the law, as soon as it becomes aware of such misuse or misappropriation of the Confidential Information in which case, the Receiving Party agrees to provide such reasonable assistance to the Disclosing Pa rty as it may require.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.5 The Receiving Party agrees to use standard of care to protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information from any misuse or misappropriation by any party and agrees to notify the Disclosing Party, to the extent permitted under the law, as soon as it becomes aware of such misuse or misappropriation of the Confidential Information in which case, the Receiving Party agrees to provide such reasonable assistance to the Disclosing Pa rty as it may require.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting 'such reasonable measures necessary... as the Receiving Party would apply to its own confidential information' from Section 2.5, the obligation on the Receiving Party becomes ambiguous. The original clause sets a benchmark for the standard of care. The removal makes it unclear what standard is expected, potentially contradicting other sections that assume a high level of protection for Confidential Information. The in-text contradiction here is the lack of clearly defined measure to the level of protection, since it was previously referring to the level of protection being provided to information which is considered of high importance.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.1 For purposes of this Agreement, "Confidential Inf ormation" shall mean information, data or material deemed proprietary by the Disclosing Party and which may be marked, or, if orally transmitted, designated as "Confidential" by the Disclosing Party and not generally known by the public or by parties which are competitive with or otherwise in an industry, trade or business similar to Disclosing Party. Confidential Information also includes any information described above which the Di sclosing Party obtains from another party and which the Disclosing Party treats as proprietary or designates in writing as Confidential Information, whether or not owned or developed by the Disclosing Party. Confidential Information includes, but is not li mited to, the following types of information and other information of a similar nature (whether or not reduced to writing): discoveries, ideas, concepts, software in various stages of development, designs, drawings, specifications, techniques, models, dat a, source code, object code, algorithms, documentation, user manuals, diagrams, flow charts, consulting methods and techniques, research, development, processes, procedures, "know -how", marketing techniques and materials, marketing and development plans, m arket analysis, customer names and other information related to customers, personal data and sensitive personal data as defined under the Personal Data Protection Act 2010, price lists, pricing policies and financial information, methods of production, use , operation and application, invented, owned or developed by the Disclosing Party as it applies to and is incorporated in Disclosing Party's proprietary software, and any patents, copyrights, trademarks existing now, for which applications may be pending o r hereafter made, acquired and granted for any of Disclosing Party's software and any improvements, enhancements or modifications thereto, Disclosing Party's physical security system, access control systems, specialized recovery equipment and techniques, a nd the details of the Disclosing Party's computer operations and recovery procedures. Confidential Information shall further include data regarding business practices, pricing, product philosophy, position relative to competitors, and review of actual del iverables of consulting projects.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 2.5 provided a clear benchmark for the standard of care expected of the Receiving Party by stating that they should use 'such reasonable measures necessary and standard of care to protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information ... as the Receiving Party would apply to its own confidential information'. The changed text omits this benchmark, making the obligation on the Receiving Party ambiguous. This omission directly contradicts the detailed definition of 'Confidential Information' in Section 1.1, which lists numerous types of highly sensitive information. Without a clear benchmark for the standard of care in Section 2.5, it is unclear how the Receiving Party is expected to protect the 'Confidential Information' as defined in Section 1.1, creating a strong in-text contradiction because the level of protection and care becomes arbitrary.

perturbation.original_text: 2.7 The term “Confidential Information ” shall not include, and the obligations of the Receiving Party in this Agreement shall not apply to any information which: (a) prior to or after the time of disclosure, becomes public knowledge and not as a result of any breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party; (b) is alrea dy in the possession of the Receiving Party at the time of disclosure prior to the time of disclosure; (c) was or comes into the possession of the Receiving Party from a third party who is not known to the Receiving Party to be bound by any obligations of con fidentiality to the Disclosing Party in respect of such information; (d) is independently developed by the Receiving Party; (e) is disclosed pursuant to a requirement, request or order of a regulatory or governmental authority or court of competent jurisdiction or to defend or prosecute a claim brought against or by or involving the Receiving Party or its Authorised Recipients but only to the extent so required, requested or ordered provided the Receiving Party agrees to notify the Disclosing Party in writing as soon as possible upon becoming aware of such requirement; and (f) both parties agree is not confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.7 The term “Confidential Information ” shall not include, and the obligations of the Receiving Party in this Agreement shall not apply to any information which: (a) prior to or after the time of disclosure, becomes public knowledge and not as a result of any breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party; (b) is alrea dy in the possession of the Receiving Party at the time of disclosure prior to the time of disclosure; (c) was or comes into the possession of the Receiving Party from a third party; (d) is independently developed by the Receiving Party; (e) is disclosed pursuant to a requirement, request or order of a regulatory or governmental authority or court of competent jurisdiction or to defend or prosecute a claim brought against or by or involving the Receiving Party or its Authorised Recipients but only to the extent so required, requested or ordered provided the Receiving Party agrees to notify the Disclosing Party in writing as soon as possible upon becoming aware of such requirement; and (f) both parties agree is not confidential.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'who is not known to the Receiving Party to be bound by any obligations of confidentiality to the Disclosing Party in respect of such information;' from Section 2.7(c), it creates an in-text contradiction with the overall purpose of the NDA. Now, even information received from a third party *bound* by confidentiality to the Disclosing Party is excluded from the definition of Confidential Information. This contradicts the intention to protect confidential information and undermines the purpose of the NDA. In-text, it is directly contradicting the whole objective of the confidentiality agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1 The Confidential Information shall be for the sole purpose of the Project and not for any other purpose and shall at all times remain the property of the Disclosing Party. Further, the Receiving Party acknowledges that nothing contained in this Agreement shall be construed as Request for Information Loans Administration System (LAS) 2 a grant of any rights to the Confidential Information by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party, other than as provided under this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section 2.7(c) removes the requirement that a third party must *not* be bound by confidentiality obligations to the Disclosing Party in order for information received from that third party to be excluded from the definition of Confidential Information. This directly contradicts the overall intention of the agreement, as stated in section 2.1, which is to protect the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information. Now, under the changed text, the Receiving Party could receive confidential information from a third party who *is* bound by confidentiality to the Disclosing Party, and that information would *not* be considered Confidential Information under the agreement, undermining the Disclosing Party's property rights and the purpose of the NDA as described in Section 2.1.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 Upon request, the Receiving Party agrees to promptly return or destroy all originals and copies of any of the Con fidential Information obtained from the Disclosing Party and thereafter the obligations of the Receiving Party under this Agreement shall be determined, save for the Receiving Party being liable for any antecedent breaches during the subsistence of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 Upon request, the Receiving Party agrees to promptly return all originals and copies of any of the Con fidential Information obtained from the Disclosing Party and thereafter the obligations of the Receiving Party under this Agreement shall be determined, save for the Receiving Party being liable for any antecedent breaches during the subsistence of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting 'or destroy' from Section 3.1, a contradiction arises. The Receiving Party is now only obligated to *return* the confidential information, not destroy it. This omission creates ambiguity, especially in the context of digital information which is easily copied. This directly creates an internal contradiction to section 2.5 which refers to the protection of the information. The clause is omitting one of the safety measures required for it.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.5 The Receiving Party agrees to use standard of care to protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information from any misuse or misappropriation by any party and agrees to notify the Disclosing Party, to the extent permitted under the law, as soon as it becomes aware of such misuse or misappropriation of the Confidential Information in which case, the Receiving Party agrees to provide such reasonable assistance to the Disclosing Pa rty as it may require.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 3.1 removes the 'or destroy' option, mandating only the return of confidential information. This directly contradicts Section 2.5, which requires the Receiving Party to protect the confidentiality of the information from misuse or misappropriation. By removing the option to destroy the information, the Receiving Party has fewer mechanisms for ensuring this protection, creating a direct conflict between the obligations defined in Section 3.1 and Section 2.5.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2 This Agreement and the obligations of the parties hereunder shall terminate (i) upon the completion of the Project (if the Receiving Party is selected for the Project) ; or (ii) on the date falling three (3 ) years from the date of this Agreement or such other date as the parties may mutually agree in writing (if the Receiving Party is not selected for the Project .
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 This Agreement and the obligations of the parties hereunder shall terminate (i) upon the completion of the Project (if the Receiving Party is selected for the Project) ; or (ii) on the date falling three (3 ) years from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'or such other date as the parties may mutually agree in writing' from the termination clause 4.2, the contract loses flexibility. This contradicts the general principle of contract law that allows parties to modify agreements by mutual consent. This creates a contradiction to 5.b. which states the agreement can be modified, the contract in Section 4.2 states the contract does not have flexibility on the termination, where in Section 5.b. is stating the opposite.
perturbation.contradicted_text: b. Any amendment, modification or variation to this Agreement may be made but subject to the written consent of both parties.
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed for the agreement to terminate on a date mutually agreed upon by both parties, providing flexibility. The changed text removes this flexibility, specifying a fixed termination date. This directly contradicts clause 'b' in the 'Contradicted Text', which states that the agreement can be modified with written consent of both parties. By eliminating the possibility of mutually agreed-upon termination dates, the changed text creates an in-text contradiction as it restricts the ability to modify the agreement's termination terms, directly conflicting with the general modification clause.

perturbation.original_text: 5.9 The Disclosing Party shall not have any liability or responsibility for errors or omissions in, or any business decisions made by the Receiving Party in reliance on, any Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.9 The Disclosing Party shall not have any liability or responsibility for errors or omissions in, or any business decisions made by the Receiving Party in reliance on, any Information disclosed under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the word 'Confidential' from Section 5.9, the protection offered to the Disclosing Party is broadened. Now, they are not liable for any information disclosed, even if it wasn't meant to be confidential. This creates a contradiction with the entire premise of the NDA, which is to protect *Confidential* Information. By removing that word, the contract now protects any information, even unintentionally disclosed public knowledge. This contradicts 1.1 which is defining the Confidential Information. Now the liability section is refering to all information that being provided, not just confidential.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.1 For purposes of this Agreement, "Confidential Inf ormation" shall mean information, data or material deemed proprietary by the Disclosing Party and which may be marked, or, if orally transmitted, designated as "Confidential" by the Disclosing Party and not generally known by the public or by parties which are competitive with or otherwise in an industry, trade or business similar to Disclosing Party. Confidential Information also includes any information described above which the Di sclosing Party obtains from another party and which the Disclosing Party treats as proprietary or designates in writing as Confidential Information, whether or not owned or developed by the Disclosing Party. Confidential Information includes, but is not li mited to, the following types of information and other information of a similar nature (whether or not reduced to writing): discoveries, ideas, concepts, software in various stages of development, designs, drawings, specifications, techniques, models, dat a, source code, object code, algorithms, documentation, user manuals, diagrams, flow charts, consulting methods and techniques, research, development, processes, procedures, "know -how", marketing techniques and materials, marketing and development plans, m arket analysis, customer names and other information related to customers, personal data and sensitive personal data as defined under the Personal Data Protection Act 2010, price lists, pricing policies and financial information, methods of production, use , operation and application, invented, owned or developed by the Disclosing Party as it applies to and is incorporated in Disclosing Party's proprietary software, and any patents, copyrights, trademarks existing now, for which applications may be pending o r hereafter made, acquired and granted for any of Disclosing Party's software and any improvements, enhancements or modifications thereto, Disclosing Party's physical security system, access control systems, specialized recovery equipment and techniques, a nd the details of the Disclosing Party's computer operations and recovery procedures. Confidential Information shall further include data regarding business practices, pricing, product philosophy, position relative to competitors, and review of actual del iverables of consulting projects.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 5.9 removes the word 'Confidential' when referring to information disclosed. This creates a contradiction with Section 1.1, which defines 'Confidential Information'. By removing the limitation of 'Confidential' in 5.9, the Disclosing Party is now not liable for *any* information disclosed, regardless of whether it meets the definition in 1.1. This directly contradicts the purpose and scope of the agreement as defined in 1.1, which specifically concerns protecting Confidential Information, meaning the change broadens the scope of the clause to include non-confidential information, thus creating a direct conflict.
